VGGT + refiner: where the limits are, and what actually moves the needle

End-to-end, training-free decomposition on the current best model (joint refiner, epoch ~76, 218349151_mri_refiner_joint/checkpoint_last.pt), breathing-val protocol, n=30. MOTION PSNR (dynamic heart voxels) is the headline metric throughout. Generated in-session; reproduce via tools/limits_decomposition.py, tools/improvements_test.py, tools/kspace_singleshot_toy.py, tools/make_limits_figures.py.

TL;DR (human-facing). I stress-tested the VGGT+refiner pipeline with a ladder of training-free "oracle" reconstructions and a 4-expert debate, and the picture is clear and actionable — it splits into two independent levers plus one transfer risk:
  • Accuracy (MOTION PSNR) is GEOMETRY-limited, not renderer-limited. With perfect motion/placement the same splat reaches 35.0 dB motion; the model sits at 20.6 dB — a ~14 dB gap that lives in motion estimation, not the splat or the refiner. (Floor = 16.7.) Most of the project's effort — refiner, SSIM — has been polishing the wrong lever.
  • Sharpness IS renderer-limited, but the culprit is the 256→518→256 RESIZE, not the splat kernel. A native-resolution splat (no resize) reaches 0.997× GT sharpness vs the current pipeline's 0.768× — the trilinear scatter itself is fine. No loss function can break this; the fix is a renderer change.
  • The real binding constraint for the stated goal (real-time transfer) is the DOMAIN GAP. Feeding true single-shot–aliased input (R=8) — what real-time actually delivers — drops the model 3.0 dB (20.6→17.6), back near the do-nothing floor. The model has never seen acquisition artifacts. And every number here is on the model's own simulator — there is no real-data metric.
Proven improvements (ranked): (1) native-resolution splat — recovers sharpness, training-free evidence; (2) multi-draw test-time ensemble — +1.8 dB motion, free; (3) degraded-input augmentation — proven necessary (the 3 dB drop) and plausible (the respiratory-sim precedent generalized). Ranked DOWN by evidence: fancier losses (sharpness is splat-limited, not loss-limited), a data-consistency loss (already ~34 dB satisfied), unfreezing the backbone (grad-norm ~0.014), and k-space input (incompatible with the frozen RGB backbone). The biggest gap is not a model change at all: a real-data, motion-region metric.

1. Method — how this was measured

The pipeline transports input pixel intensities: world_points = scanner_coords + Δ → trilinear splat into a 12×256×256 cube → V_canon → 3D-UNet refiner → V_refined, scored against the target-phase volume. To find where the error is, I built a ladder of reconstructors that each removes one source of error, all scored identically:

  • identity — Δ=0 splat of the (breathing-corrupted) input slices. The do-nothing floor.
  • model V_canon / V_refined — the trained joint model. The operating point.
  • oracle (perfect placement) — splat the true target-phase planes at the model's z's with Δ=0. This is an upper bound: it hands the model perfect motion correction AND perfect appearance. (Honest caveat in §3.1: a pure-warp model can't fully reach it.)
  • oracle native-256 / nearest / super-2× — the same perfect-placement splat but varying the renderer (skip the 518 resize / drop the trilinear tent / use a 512 grid) to attribute the sharpness loss.

Plus three diagnostics: data-consistency (re-slice the recon at its input geometry vs the input pixels), an out-of-distribution breathing test (re-score under a respiratory waveform the model never trained on — a sim-overfit check), and a single-shot k-space test (feed R=8-aliased input). I also ran a 4-expert debate (MRI physicist · neural-rendering architect · pragmatic experimentalist · red-team skeptic); their convergence and disagreements shaped the experiments and are summarized in §4.

2. The operating point

reconstructorMOTION PSNR ↑bbox PSNRsharp/GT
identity (floor)16.6623.320.633
model V_canon19.6527.130.648
model V_refined (best)20.6228.650.673
oracle (perfect placement)34.9841.840.768

The refiner adds +0.97 dB motion over the raw splat — consistent with reports 10–12. But the headline is the last row: perfect placement is 14 dB above the model.

3. Limitations (with the toy experiment that proves each)

3.1 — The dominant gap is MOTION ESTIMATION, not the renderer (~14 dB)

With the same splat and no refiner, perfect motion/placement reaches 35.0 dB motion — so the renderer can represent the dynamic heart far better than the model currently extracts. The model's 20.6 dB is bottlenecked by getting the displacement field right, which matches doc 07's finding that the model under-corrects (DVF fit slope 0.42, plateaus at 7–8 mm vs ~24 mm applied).

Honest caveat (red-team). The oracle feeds the true target-phase planes, so it also hands the model appearance a pure-warp transport can't synthesize (ES blood-pool, through-plane disocclusion). So the full 14 dB is not all reachable by better Δ — part is the warp-only wall and part is the information the model is contractually denied (no input cardiac t, no respiratory r → forced regression-to-mean shrinkage). The achievable ceiling sits between 21 and 35 dB. But the direction is unambiguous: the lever is motion/geometry, and the model is well below even a conservative ceiling.

3.2 — The sharpness ceiling is the RESIZE, not the kernel or the loss

Reports 08/12 attributed the blur to "the splat" and showed neither L1 nor SSIM breaks ~0.69× GT. This decomposition pinpoints why: at perfect placement, the current 518→256 splat gives 0.768×, but splatting at native 256 (no resize) gives 0.997× — near-perfect. Nearest-neighbour matches native trilinear (0.997), so the trilinear tent is innocent; a 512 grid only partly helps (0.801). The 256→518→256 resampling round-trip is where the high-frequency detail dies. This is the strongest single result in the report: it converts "the splat is lossy, live with it" into a concrete, fixable renderer bug (see §5, Improvement A).

3.3 — Coverage caps at 12 planes, but it's not the motion bottleneck

The splat fills only ~70% of cube voxels, and S is hard-capped at min(T=12, bbox_z, img_per_seq) ⇒ you cannot feed >12 input planes without enlarging the canonical grid. But the dynamic heart lives in well-covered central planes, and the perfect-placement oracle reaches 35 dB at the same 71% coverage — so coverage is not what caps motion PSNR. Multi-draw ensembling (§5-B) confirms: unioning coverage over draws buys only modest motion PSNR. Coverage is a full/bbox problem, not a motion problem.

3.4 — The real binding constraint: the domain gap (and no real-data metric)

Everything above is measured on clean simulated data. The project's goal is gated→real-time transfer, and real-time gives single-shot, prospectively-undersampled frames. Feeding R=8-aliased input drops the model 3.0 dB motion (20.6→17.6) — within ~1 dB of the do-nothing floor. The model is brittle to the artifacts that define its target domain. Combined with the fact that no clean 3-D real-data reference exists (OCMR, report 13, is k-t-reconstructed and unscored), this — not the splat — is the project's true risk.

Reassuring counter-evidence (sim-overfit is NOT happening, within the motion family). A real worry was that the model just memorized the training respiratory waveform. It did not: under an out-of-distribution respiratory model (different waveform shape, larger amplitude, more direction jitter), the model's gain over identity survives and grows (+4.0 dB in-distribution → +4.9 dB OOD). It learned to correct motion, not to replay a simulator. The untested gap is the non-rigid single-shot physics, not the breathing parameters.

3.5 — Two more limits worth noting (and what they rule OUT)

Data consistency is already satisfied. Re-slicing the model's recon at its own input geometry gives 34 dB — the splat round-trips its own pixels well, so a data-consistency loss (a popular suggestion) has little gradient to give. The frozen backbone is not the bottleneck: even in the joint run the aggregator's grad-norm is ~0.014 vs the point head's ~0.6 — unfreezing 605M params (2.8× slower) barely moves them. Both are evidence-based kills, not endorsements.

4. The expert debate (4 agents, independent)

I ran four adversarial subagents. Their convergence was striking: all three "builder" agents independently nominated a training-free decomposition as the highest-value next step (the physicist's data-consistency probe, the architect's resize-vs-kernel split, the pragmatist's coverage/ensemble sweep) — which is exactly what this report executes. Key positions:

AgentTop pickStrongest kill
MRI physicistData-consistency loss (r/t-blind-safe)k-space input — kills own field's idea (frozen RGB backbone)
Rendering architectFeature-splat + tiny decoder (break warp-only wall)Unfreezing backbone (0.014 grad); splat-free decode = ablation not product
PragmatistMore slices / coverage + multi-draw ensembleFancy/perceptual losses — "a trap; sharpness is splat-limited"
Red-team(critique) — the oracle bbox number is static-tissue-inflated; report MOTIONRefiner/SSIM will disappoint most; biggest miss = no real-data metric

The experiments adjudicated the disagreements: the red-team was right that motion PSNR (not bbox) is the honest number — I led with it, and the gap survived (14 dB). The physicist's DC loss is real but low-headroom here (34 dB already). The architect's resize hypothesis was confirmed (0.768→0.997). The pragmatist's "fancy losses are a trap" is corroborated (sharpness is the renderer, not the loss).

5. Proven improvement directions (ranked by evidence)

A — Native-resolution splat (attacks sharpness) — PROVEN at oracle + model level

The fix for §3.2: splat the native-256 intensity at native-resolution positions instead of the 518-upsampled intensity. With the model's own (imperfect) geometry, no retraining, sharpness rises 0.648→0.721 (and the oracle ceiling jumps to 0.997). Cost: decouple the splat resolution from the DINOv2 518 input + a head re-fit (the head was trained against the 518 splat). Counter: sharpness is the secondary metric — this buys crispness, not the 14 dB of motion PSNR. Ship it, but it's not the headline lever.

B — Multi-draw test-time ensemble (attacks motion variance) — PROVEN, free

Average V_refined over K independent scattered draws of the same subject/phase: 20.62→22.38 dB motion at K=8 (+1.77). Zero training, pure inference. Caveats: it blurs (averaging lowers sharpness), it costs K× latency (a tension with "real-time"), and the modest size confirms coverage/variance is not the main gap. Use it as a free baseline-raiser and as proof that the residual error is genuine motion estimation, not variance.

C — Degraded-input augmentation (attacks the domain gap) — PROVEN necessary

§3.4 shows a 3 dB cliff on single-shot input. The respiratory-sim precedent (doc 05) shows the model can learn to correct a simulated corruption and even generalize it (§3.4 OOD result). So adding single-shot k-space undersampling + transient/SNR to the training augmentation is the highest-leverage transfer fix — same recipe that made breathing "decisive." This is the one that matters for the stated goal.

Ranked DOWN by evidence (don't fund yet)

  • Fancier / perceptual / adversarial losses — sharpness is renderer-limited (§3.2); no objective breaks a ceiling the resize sets. Fix the renderer first.
  • Data-consistency loss — already ~34 dB satisfied (§3.5); little gradient to give.
  • Unfreeze the aggregator — grad-norm 0.014; 2.8× cost for ~no movement.
  • k-space / complex input — incompatible with the frozen RGB DINOv2 patch-embed; high cost, overlaps C.
  • More input planes (S>12) — capped by the canonical grid; coverage isn't the motion bottleneck (§3.3).

6. The single most important thing NOT being measured

(Red-team's call, and I agree.) Every metric here — including mine — is on the model's own simulator, and bbox/full PSNR is dominated by static tissue. The project cannot currently distinguish "a working slice-to-volume reconstructor" from "a plausible-GIF generator." The missing instrument: a real-data, motion-region metric — reconstruct a held-out multi-frame reference per slice from OCMR (or any real RT cine), feed the held-out single frames, and score psnr_motion at matched phases against a trivial baseline (nearest-acquired-frame, conditional-mean). Build this before chasing more in-distribution dB.

7. Recommended sequence

  1. Build the real-data motion metric (§6) — without it, every dB is suspect.
  2. Degraded-input augmentation (Improvement C) — the transfer lever; cheap, precedented.
  3. Attack motion estimation (the 14 dB lever): low-rank/basis-DVF motion subspace on the point head (architect's regularizer for the under-constrained through-plane Δ), and/or content-inferred cardiac-phase conditioning. This is the dominant accuracy lever and is still largely untried.
  4. Native-resolution splat (Improvement A) — ship the sharpness win with a head re-fit.
  5. Multi-draw ensemble (Improvement B) — free baseline raiser.

Future training experiments this report scoped but did not run (shared-cluster time): a feature-splat + tiny 3-D decoder overfit (architect's test of breaking the warp-only wall), and a degraded-input fine-tune to confirm C recovers the 3 dB cliff.

Reproduce: tools/limits_decomposition.py (ladder + OOD + DC), tools/improvements_test.py (native splat + multi-draw), tools/kspace_singleshot_toy.py --model (domain gap), tools/make_limits_figures.py (figures), _html/build_limits_report.py (this page). All numbers n=30, breathing-val, joint refiner ckpt epoch ~76.
